title: SEGMENT 13 — Cord blood transplant: small dose, easier match

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Cord blood transplant: small dose, easier match
Let me return briefly to cord blood as a transplant source, because it has its own remarkable history and a particular role in the modern landscape.
The first cord blood transplant was done in 1988 by Eliane Gluckman in Paris, treating a six-year-old boy with Fanconi anemia using cord blood from his newborn sister.

He survived, his bone marrow was reconstituted by his sister's cord cells, and the proof of principle was established: the small amount of blood remaining in a placenta after birth contained enough HSCs to rebuild a child's hematopoietic system.
This launched an entire industry of cord blood banking.

Public cord banks — funded by governments, foundations, and the Be The Match registry — accept donated cord blood units, HLA-type them, store them in liquid nitrogen, and make them available to any matched patient worldwide.
Private cord banks store units at parents' expense for potential future use by the family.
There are now hundreds of thousands of cord blood units banked globally.
The key clinical property of cord blood is immunological naivety.
The T cells in a cord blood unit have never seen any antigen — they haven't been educated yet.
This means they're less likely to mount a severe GVHD response and, importantly, they tolerate higher degrees of HLA mismatch than adult donor cells do.
A cord blood unit matched at 4 out of 6 HLA loci can engraft successfully, whereas an adult unrelated donor at the same match level would be far too risky.
This makes cord blood especially valuable for ethnic minority patients underrepresented in adult registries, and for patients of mixed ancestry who genuinely have no matched adult donor in the world.
Cord blood is also available off the shelf — once banked, you can ship it overnight rather than waiting weeks for an adult donor workup.
The limitation is dose.
A single cord unit contains roughly one-tenth the HSCs of an adult marrow or PBSC harvest.
For pediatric recipients this is plenty; for adults it can be marginal.
Engraftment is slower — sometimes 30 to 40 days — and graft failure rates are higher.

Strategies to address the dose problem include double cord transplants (using two units), ex vivo expansion of cord HSCs with molecules like StemRegenin-1 or UM171, and intra-bone delivery to place the cells directly in the marrow.
The recently approved cord blood product Omisirge is an ex-vivo-expanded cord unit designed to engraft faster than conventional cord.
Cord blood as a transplant source has receded somewhat as haploidentical transplant has expanded — when every patient can find a family donor, the unique access value of cord blood narrows.

But cord blood remains essential for patients with no family donor and is increasingly important as a source for off-the-shelf cellular therapy products being developed for cancer and autoimmune disease.
